body:
## UX goal
Create a planning-first outline corkboard that feels as immediate as drafting: writers should be able to scan beats, make fast edits, reorder confidently, and jump straight into the exact scene with one click.

## Information hierarchy
1. Story context and mode controls (story title + Editor/Corkboard switch) remain topmost.
2. Board controls: scene count, status filter, and quick-add beat CTA.
3. Scene cards: title (primary), intent + POV (secondary), short summary (tertiary), status tag (actionable metadata).
4. Empty-state guidance: first 3 beats prompt + create action.

## Layout and components
- Keep current page shell and mode pattern in `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` with the existing `ViewModeSwitch`.
- In corkboard mode, retain sticky board toolbar pattern from `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.module.css` and use right-side toolbar space (`toolbarRight`) for filter + add actions.
- Card layout should extend `SceneCard` content hierarchy:
  - Top row: drag handle, title, chapter/sequence label.
  - Middle metadata row: `Intent`, `POV` as compact text chips (not heavy pill UI).
  - Body: 1-3 line summary clamp.
  - Footer: status selector/tag.
- Card click should navigate to the corresponding scene in editor (same story route, anchored scene target).
- Empty board should include a primary CTA (`Create first beat`) plus suggested starter beats (Act 1 setup, first turn, commitment).

## Interaction states
- Idle: board shows scene count + active filter, cards fully interactive.
- Loading: keep existing skeleton card pattern, include disabled toolbar controls.
- Empty: actionable onboarding with quick-start beats and no dead-end copy.
- Error: inline banner at top of board area with retry action; never block view switch.
- Success: after reorder/status/edit, show subtle transient confirmation (non-modal) and keep focus in context.

## Accessibility
- Keyboard: ensure all card actions are keyboard reachable (open, reorder fallback controls, status change, quick add).
- Focus: preserve visible focus rings using existing tokenized focus style (`--color-carbon-600`) and return focus predictably after navigation/save.
- Contrast: keep text/border/background combinations within grayscale token contrast targets; avoid relying on color alone for status meaning.
- Text clarity: status names should be writer-language (`Planned`, `Drafting`, `Final`) and intent/POV labels should be explicit.

## Visual style
- Continue current grayscale design system from `src/styles/tokens.css`; do not introduce new brand colors for this ticket.
- Keep cards calm and editorial: white surface, `fashion-300` border, shadow-on-hover, restrained metadata typography.
- Emphasize hierarchy through spacing and type weight, not decorative effects.
- Toolbar and board background should keep current white-on-polar layering for strong scanability.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop: multi-column auto-fill grid remains primary planning surface.
- Tablet: preserve 2-ish column behavior with readable card width; toolbar controls wrap without overlap.
- Mobile: single-column cards; toolbar stacks vertically; primary actions remain one-tap and visible without horizontal scroll.

## Codebase alignment
- Reviewed and aligned with existing architecture: `src/app/layout.tsx`, `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx`, `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`, `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`, `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`, and token/CSS modules.
- Current implementation already supports mode switching, drag reorder, status update, and card click callback wiring.
- Structural gap to call out: scene model in `src/types/scene.ts` and mock persistence in `src/lib/scenes.ts` currently lacks `intent` and `pov` fields required by this ticket.

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse:
  - `ViewModeSwitch` for one-click mode change.
  - `Corkboard` drag-and-drop stack and sticky toolbar shell.
  - `SceneCard` as base card primitive and status control pattern.
  - Existing grayscale tokens and spacing scale.
- New build:
  - Lightweight metadata row for intent/POV on cards.
  - Status filter controls in toolbar (`toolbarRight`).
  - Empty-state onboarding block with first-3-beats assist.
  - Scene deep-link behavior from card click into editor scene target.

## Feasibility notes
- Reorder semantics should remain deterministic (single source of truth order index) to avoid board/editor mismatch.
- Deep-linking depends on editor scene anchoring support; if scene anchors are unavailable, add explicit fallback selection behavior to avoid broken navigation.
- Status filtering must be non-destructive view filtering only (no data mutation).
- LocalStorage-backed mock data in current code suggests metadata edits need schema migration handling for existing stored scenes.

## Constraints / implementation notes
- Keep design compatible with the current route shape: `/story/[id]` with `editor|corkboard` mode switching inside the same page.
- Preserve current interaction speed expectations: quick-capture and mode switching should remain one click/tap with no modal gate.
- Anchor likely implementation touchpoints:
  - `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` (mode state, click navigation, filter state wiring)
  - `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx` + `.module.css` (toolbar controls, empty/error/success states)
  - `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx` + `.module.css` (intent/POV presentation, card hierarchy updates)
  - `src/types/scene.ts` and `src/lib/scenes.ts` (metadata fields + persistence updates)

## Handoff
Assign to: Founding Engineer

## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
Outline/corkboard is expected for planning-oriented writers, but we can differentiate with tighter linkage between plan and draft execution (card-to-scene jump, status filtering, and minimal friction edits) so planning never becomes a separate tool.

## Competitor evidence
- Living Writer treats outline + manuscript structure as a core writing flow, validating this as a category expectation: https://livingwriter.com/features
- Campfire offers rich planning/worldbuilding modules, but many writers experience feature sprawl; this creates room for a faster, drafting-first outline surface: https://www.campfirewriting.com/
- Inkitt emphasizes publish/read feedback loops; it is less focused on private pre-draft planning workflows: https://writer.inkitt.com

## Risks / trade-offs
- If card metadata is too dense, planning overhead can outweigh drafting momentum.
- Tight scene coupling can be brittle if reorder semantics are unclear.
- Without clear status definitions, filters become noise.

## Recommendation
Approve. Ask Design to prioritize low-friction card editing and unmistakable plan-to-draft navigation so this feels intentionally lighter than competitor planning boards.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design

## Market Research Review Request

Please approve, filter, or reframe this ticket using differentiation against Living Writer/Campfire and writer-first workflow clarity.

- Source goal: Write a story writing app
- Requested output: decision + differentiation note + any scope adjustments before engineering
